comments.text: The lower bound for this problem can be improved from four to five: there are infinitely many $n$ with at least five divisors in $(\sqrt{n}, \sqrt{n} + 31 n^{1/4})$, hence at least ten in the symmetric window $(\sqrt{n} - 31 n^{1/4}, \sqrt{n} + 31 n^{1/4})$. So any absolute constant $K$ here must satisfy $K \ge 5$. The witnesses are\[ n = (x^2-1)(x^2-4)(x^2-16)(x^2-49) \]for solutions of the Pell equation $z^2 - 56x^2 = 65$ with $x \ge 37$ (infinitely many: the orbit of $(x,z)=(37,277)$ under $(x,z) \mapsto (15x+2z,\ 112x+15z)$ gives $x = 37, 1109, 33233, \ldots$; a second seed gives $x = 64, 1918, \ldots$; the solutions below $37$ are exactly $x \in \{1,2,4,7\}$, each of which annihilates a factor of $n$). The five divisors are\[ d_1 = (x^2-4)(x^2-1), \quad d_2 = (x^2-16)(x^2-1), \quad d_3 = (x^2-16)(x^2-4), \]\[ d_4 = (x+7)(x-4)(x-2)(x-1), \quad d_5 = x^4 - 35x^2 + 56 + 2xz. \]The first four are the balanced factorizations of the octic — this is exactly the Erdős–Rosenfeld construction [ErRo97, Prop. 4.2], in symmetric form, and by their Prop. 4.3 four is the maximum that splitting eight factors can give. The fifth pair exists exactly on the Pell conic, via the polynomial identity\[ (x^4 - 35x^2 + 56)^2 - n = 4x^2(56x^2 + 65), \]so $z^2 = 56x^2+65$ makes the right side the perfect square $(2xz)^2$ and $d_5 e_5 = n$ with $d_5 - e_5 = 4xz$. Writing $u_i = d_i + n/d_i = 2x^4 - 70x^2 + \kappa_i$ with $(\kappa_i) = (788, 212, 113, -112, 112)$ shows the five pairs are distinct for every $x$; one-sidedness and the window inclusion follow from $d - \sqrt{n} < y/2 + y^2/(8\sqrt{n})$ with $y_i = 60x^2-780,\ 36x^2-180,\ 30x^2+15,\ 180x,\ 4xz$ and $n^{1/4} > x^2 - 49$, which gives the certificate for all $x \ge 41$; there are no Pell solutions with $38 \le x \le 45$, and $x = 37$ is checked exactly. The constant $31$ is minimal for this family ($u_1 - 2\sqrt{n} > 900 = 30^2$ always). A short note with the complete proof, the exact per-pair thresholds, and a standalone verification script is at: https://czechu.blog/erdos/problems/887/note887.pdf Credits and honest scope. The two ingredients are individually known: the four balanced pairs are Erdős–Rosenfeld's own construction, and the idea of forcing an extra near-$\sqrt{n}$ factorization by a Pell condition appears in Chan's work on perfect squares (arXiv:1303.2069, three divisors for squares on $X^2-2Y^2=2$). What is new is stacking the Pell pair on top of a maximal system of balanced splits (maximal by [ErRo97, Prop. 4.3] itself), which lifts the count from four to five. Three caveats: (1) I claim nothing below the window constant $31$ — at small $c$ the Erdős–Rosenfeld four (which live at $c \approx 8$–$16$; the one-sided $c=1$ phrasing of the site's summary caps at two divisors by the $u$-argument and has been formally refuted in the formal-conjectures repository, PR #1942) is untouched. (2) Large family members appear to have exactly five window divisors (complete enumeration at four members up to 48 digits); only $\ge 5$ is proved. (3) The question itself — whether $K$ exists — remains open; a sixth pair within this framework would need two simultaneous Pell conditions, generically a genus $\ge 1$ curve, so each such family contributes only finitely many members by Siegel, which weakly supports the existence of $K$. Provenance: the construction was found by an AI-assisted search; every identity and inequality was then re-derived and verified independently (symbolically and in exact arithmetic, with members checked up to 84 digits), and the proof in the note is short enough to check by hand.
[Le25] by P. Letendre seem to have some partial results on this problem.
Exactly that paper had also been helpful in improving the asymptotic exponent in Erdos #873 from $1/3$ to $1/4$.

Tsz Ho Chan (https://eudml.org/doc/279082, https://arxiv.org/pdf/1406.2230) has studied this problem. They have shown this conjecture when $n$ is a perfect square or near a perfect square. In general, the problem reduces to showing that if $Q_1(x, y), \cdots, Q_K(x, y)$ are certain polynomials of degree $1$, then there exists only finitely many $(x, y)$ such that they are simultaneously perfect squares. This should be correct under Bomberi-Lang, though unconditionally it appears very hard. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
